item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was nicely done. Reminds me of Junji Ito's "The Thing that Drifted Ashore" and has the same sense of dread attached to it in my opinion (hopefully a dozen people haven't said the same thing) which is a compliment coming from an Ito nut.
This one took a while to grow on me, but I thoroughly enjoyed it.

item.title: In my opinion, everywhere!
item.content:encoded:
The rating is over +1800 as of now so I am obviously out of step with most who read the scp. I've been reading the site since before series so that's not a surprise1. On the other hand I find scp-3001 to be top-notch. I offer the following to explain my downvote.

In my opinion, the format is poorly designed and the non-narrative text too verbose. It is enough to be distracting. Discovery, Atzak Protocol, and the other addendum should be in collapsed sections, including moving the log of the initial discovery to it's own section. In practical terms, collapsed sections help a reader concentrate on the containment and description while also providing an outline to guide the reader through the rest of the scp2. In universe, the entry is almost 50 years old, central to the Foundation so constantly reviewed, and I'd expect the format to reflect it. In my opinion it is basically a minor departure from the normal format making it harder to read. That can elevate many entries but I don't see how it does in this case.

+ show block
– hide block

The power of the article is contained in the personal impact as shown through the logs and diaries. The SCP itself, after 3 series, is interesting but not exceptional. The main text says it is scary when that's the job of the logs. It is wordy and contains extraneous details. That's what I mean by too verbose. For example, from the Special Containment Procedures:

Individuals affected by the anomalous properties of SCP-3000 are to be held in containment indefinitely.
…
There is currently no known cure for exposure to SCP-3000; as such, affected individuals should be contained and quarantined for further evaluation.

The two halves of the second sentence are, respectively, misplaced and redundant. "No known cure"3 should be part of the description; affected individuals are already contained.

SCP-3000 was discovered in 1971, shortly after two Bangladeshi fishing boats and fifteen fishermen were reported missing after drifting near the Indian coast.

I think it could be condensed to something like "SCP-3000 was discovered in 1971 after two Bangladeshi fishing boats and fifteen fishermen were reported missing near the Indian coast." Some readers are going to try to keep in mind "shortly after" and "drifting" in case those are important details. Individually minor but I think significant over the whole article.

Why is the SCP classified Level 5? In universe, only 151-HOLLISTER ATZAK PROTOCOL and resulting object class thaumiel are sensitive. Usually that's the part locked under [INPUT LEVEL 5/3000 SECURITY CREDENTIALS]. Stylistically it is one more layer to discover, sacrificing D-class like water for a fundamental tool of the Foundation based on an unexplained SCP4.


As a courtesy to our readers on mobile devices, please collapse long posts. ~Zyn
Footnotes
1. Get off my lawn!
2. The substantial part of this entry.
3. "Currently" is implied.
4. That's never gone wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is an extremely good article except for one thing: why the HELL would you use a picture of Aziz Ansari as the doctor?? At the very least, if you really want a picture of the guy (which seems kind of unnecessary), find some stock model no one's ever heard of. Literally anyone who's watched Parks and Recreation, or anything else Ansari's been in, will recognize him.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very good.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Honestly, I think this is my favorite SCP.

I am an atheist, I always thought when you die you flow into the void, absolute non-existence. I never thought that to be a frightening thought, why be afraid of something of a state where you don't even exist.

This skip put that fear back into me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a pretty bold comment to just say. I've never played any of the Mass Effect games.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i've played me2 like 4 times, and the similarities pointed out are like… inevitable when you're writing about huge eldritch bastards that fuck with your mind. and that on its own is not a particularly unique premise; it's been done countless times in countless different ways, and i think that the specific log of the marriage is a coincidence - maybe loose inspiration at best. i don't think any of the other similarities would've even been noticed if that particular specific example was not the same across both pieces of media.

EDIT: expounding on the above, there is basically no correlation between the actual entities beyond them being big creatures whom fuck with your memory. the Reaper is a gigantic sentient synthetic-organic spaceship that wants to harvest all organic life, the eel is literally just a big fucking eel that happens to enjoy the occasional person in its diet

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0AP1bC-R_1k&t=5m30s

The entire situation is lifted directly from the backstory of the reaper IFF mission. Science team gets sent by shadow organization to investigate eldritch horror, eldritch horror fucks with their memories, *specific log regarding shared memories of a marriage*, everyone in the vicinity gets hollowed out. The only difference is the reaper is dead and Anantashesha is merely sedentary. It's even implied that Cerberus gets its amnestics from the reaper research.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: So we're just gonna forget the dive team and the guy that went out of the submarine, all seeing this huge creature open its jaws and eat them alive, and the fact there's tons of human bodies in its stomach.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love this. Every bit of it. It's a long article but I love that too. It's like a slow descent into insanity. That plot twist where it's revealed the therapist's memories were being replaced this whole time, the idea of this creature showing us how easily we can be turned into nothing and forgotten (something that's one of my darkest fears), and the fact that SCP-3000 is literally only doing it just to hunt. If this article doesn't show the true brutality and uncaring erasure of existence, and how unimportant one human really is… I don't know what can.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Grazie!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Folks, I'm glad to announce that djkaktus's Joreth's and A Random Day's collaborative masterpiece has now been translated into italian. For those interested, link here SCP-3000 Italian Translation.

Thanks again for the nightmares, pals.

item.content:encoded:
This thing gave me a freaking nightmare last night.

+1

item.content:encoded:
Thogurus this has been done and it failed

Like the 500 other entries in THAT GODDAM LIZARDS TESTING LOG
on the bright side I have read my 3000th scp article, I am starting to reach the point where my brain cant handle all the information

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's like a black hole, absorbing data like light, I suppose. Yes, it likely is a purposeful reference.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it called Y-909 because of SCP-909? I believe that's Mr. Forgetful. I dunno if thats been said already, this is a large sea of comments to search.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Big eel would like you to get closer.
[Allow] [Allow]
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm kind of bored with 'Lovecraftian' stuff for the most part because a lot of writers don't seem to get what cosmic horror is actually meant to be and think it's all just tentacles and junk about incomprehensible monsters.

This article showcases what Cosmic Horror is really supposed to be about.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, it's not just the veil (though that's part of it) many, many SCPs will kill you/trap your consciousness in your rotting corpse for all eternity/teleport you to the Pacific Ocean and eat you/kill everybody if you don't get amnestics.

Also, I like the Foundation to be antiheroic over being purely the 'good guys' but that's just my view.

item.title: Re: Of the nagas, I am Ananta.
item.content:encoded: *Day Breaking Noises*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A lot of people on this thread have said they don’t find a lot of fear in this, and that’s fine. Fear is subjective. That being said, this really, really, really worked for me. It combines my fear of the dark, fear of deep water, fear of deep dark water, fear of being forgotten, fear of losing my identity, fear of someone confusing my identity, and so much more. Add all the clever references to some other amazing skips (Tabula Rasa caught me off guard) and you got yourself what is the perfect SCP-3000. +1.

item.title: ICE COLD!
item.content:encoded: I love André 3- I mean… SCP 3000! Easily one of my favorites. Side note, I'm curious if I'm the first one to see this or just the first one to respond. Either way, the joke is greatly appreciated.

item.title: NOW WHAT'S COOLER THAN BEING COOL
I love this！＋1
item.content:encoded: Thank you kaktus!

item.title: Re: love it
item.content:encoded:
am i exaggerating? absolutely
but maybe im not

item.title: love it
item.content:encoded: truly one of my favourites, perhaps the favourite, tied with 3001. VERY well written, comparable to monoliths of literature

H E R C U L E A N

E E L

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: good winner in this 3k game.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
it definitely feels like it was designed with the idea that longer is better

That's where you're wrong, kiddo.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Death of one's identity is one of the scariest things I can think of and giant, horrific monsters lurking at the depths of the ocean are pretty high up there too, but this article did nothing for me.

The SCP itself feels almost incidental to the article. It's basically just an amnestics vending machine to serve as a vehicle for the set of journals at the bottom, which feels like an underutilization of a cool monster and leaves the story itself diluted by how long the preamble to it is.

Nothing is badly written of course, but it definitely feels like it was designed with the idea that longer is better (and to be fair judging by the score, that seems to be the site's preference).

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Absolutely enjoyable, I binged this one and enjoyed every last second of it.
A friend of mine recommended this to me and at first I thought that this is too long for my attention, and I've never been more glad to be so wrong.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: http://fuji.enicra.com:2000/exp.html

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Errors/Corrections
– hide block

Article is locked, so I can't make the following fixes:
Article is not locked, so I made the following fixes:

  • "Turn on your lights boys, Here we go." --> "Turn on your lights boys, here we go."
  • "Total radio silence for 30 seconds." This is the only instance where the number of seconds is not spelled out.
  • "Personnel on-board the…" This is the only time "onboard" is hyphenated.
  • "...but I was so angry at him that I tore them up front of him." -->"in front"
  • "...it's likely his belongings will just be disposed of, and is now technically Foundation property." --> "are now"
  • "0.7 km" This is the only time the metric is formatted with a space between it and the number.
Nitpicks
– hide block

Here are issues I found that didn't affect my verdict:

  • Inconsistencies in the italicizing of the Foundation submersibles, especially the Stravinsky.
  • As someone else pointed out, Foxtrot doesn't sound off with the others. Starting to think that maybe this was intentional, since it's been pointed out since June of this year, but since there's no reply to that comment, it bothers me and I think it's an oversight.
  • "front-facing headlamps"-->I would recommend "anterior" for tone purposes.
  • The alternating between using "-" and "—" is distracting. (Flipping from one to the other happens line by line, and even once in the same line in the initial exploration log.)
  • What was the picture of Venkat's wife and daughter doing in Dr. Mannaya's office in the first place?
  • Dr. Mannava and Venkat would have to both not recognize that it is Venkat in the picture with his wife and kid. While the effects of SCP-3000 are clearly powerful, surreptitious, and already in effect, I have a hard time believing this, especially when Dr. Mannava can recognize Venkat's handwriting, something way more subtle than his face.
  • If the Foundation personnel archives explain clearly enough that Venkat had this family, why did the director indicate that his belongings would probably be disposed of on account that Venkat hadn't maintained contact with them?
  • "Being briefed on its effects doesn't change the fact that I need to constantly keep tabs on all staff, myself included, and ground us to reality." Dr. Mannava seems like he would have used the Foundation personnel archives as a resource for this purpose well before the incident with the photograph.
  • Addendum 6 is directed to the questions of new recruits, I assume for their education on SCP-3000, but it's Level-5 classified too.
  • "[SCP-3000 is]… anomalous, maybe, but not really that extraordinary." (brackets mine) I'm sorry, what?

I think the strengths and weaknesses of the article have been articulated very well by the readers, as usual. In addition to the praise, I'll say that the replay value for the article is great (e.g. lines like ""Of course, one saving grace of being on the psychological division for the Atzak Project is the awareness of its potential effects - I'm aware of what's happening to my psyche." — Dr. Mannava), the character building is effective, the 6th addendum is creepy as hell, there are some great lines in here ("…like my body reacting to reflexes it didn't know it had."), and the twist, while not perfect, is as good of one as you'll see on the site. I would be proud to point first-time readers to this article.

item.title: Re: Provenance
item.content:encoded: The picture of Dr. Krishnamoorthy has been deleted from the referenced page on Wikimedia Commons and no longer exists.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: scary eel, made me think +1

item.title: Re: I'm not skeptical about this skip anymore
item.content:encoded:
Well, I was even considering downvoting this when I first wrote this reply because of what I said before. It still makes SCP-3000 look a bit underwhelming as a horror-inducing monster to me, to be honest.
but since…

a. that was your point
b. there's no reason why the foundation mustn't use them
c. Nox's message says that they have no other choice clearly enough

So.. yeah. I'll change my mind and upvote this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think this would be spookier if some of or all of the bodies in the eel were still alive. They could be stated to be braindead or that could be left ambiguous; I just think the fact that the eel extracts their “soul” makes a good opportunity for that type of horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: 2419 is Euclid because it’s filled with thousands of psychopathic zombies that are constantly trying to escape. The eel is Thaumiel because it lazes about at the bottom of the ocean and provides amnestics in exchange for occasionally feeding it one person who isn’t going to come back. Ever.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Big fihs be afraid of meeh!~ I don't think it's worthy candidate for 3000, but I have to respect others people choice. Btw not Thaumiel at all, scp-2419 doing the same thing and it's only Euclid.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a long SCP, but absolutely worth it. All I can say is that it is beautifully twisted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Foxtrot doesn't check in on the log for some reason.

item.title: Re: I'm very skeptical about this skip
item.content:encoded:
Hey look you got it.

Coincidentally, this is also what Atzak is about.

item.title: Re: I'm very skeptical about this skip
item.content:encoded:
which looks like it's there just to make this Thaumiel to me.

No, I think it is there because:

It's like I'm seeing the Foundation full of exploitation instinct that is a monster worse than SCP-3000 itself.

Was the intent.

item.title: I'm very skeptical about this skip
item.content:encoded:
A massive eel that wipes memory, eats people and creates barrels of thing that also wipes memory does indeed make me shudder in fear, since losing memories, forgetting what I am, going insane and dying(which can actually happen in real life because of dementia and such) is one of my biggest fear.

On the other hand, the foundation turning those barrels of thing that wipes memory from a massive eel that wipes memory eats people and creates barrels of that very thing into a amnestics ingredient? not so much. It's like I'm seeing the Foundation full of exploitation instinct that is a monster worse than SCP-3000 itself.

Don't get me wrong though, it's not that I hate this skip… it's more like I like it better without using Y-909 for amnestics parts, which looks like it's there just to make this Thaumiel to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
the eel
is all

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not quite the same, unfortunately, considering this one is a collab and the other is a rewrite of an article that was already +1000. But a neat factoid, nonetheless.

E E L

item.title: Re: ALRIGHT ALRIGHT ALRIGHT ALRIGHT

Cause the thought alone is killing me right now

item.title: ALRIGHT ALRIGHT ALRIGHT ALRIGHT
item.content:encoded: ALERT : SCP-3000 has undergone severe changes and as such its description has been wholly overhauled.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It was such a good read that I made an account just to upvote it.
